Check out the satellite feeds on the Ras Laffan thermal footprint. 

The complex is rated for a 77 mtpa nameplate, but the data from July 7th and July 12th shows thermal signals lighting up 6 trains—putting active online liquefaction at a measly 34.7 mtpa.

The remaining 8 trains are showing zero thermal heat. Plus, we already know RasGas Trains 4 and 6 are total write-offs for the foreseeable future after taking those direct missile hits back in March—the street's saying it’s a multi-year repair job.

Just to map out the macro tape, ever since the geopolitical friction boiled over on July 11th, not a single LNG carrier has managed to push through the SoH.

#oott #iran

"If" the US pulls the trigger on blockade refresh, Tehran is officially back to running a race against the clock.

Kpler numbers show Iran’s onshore crude storage has cleared out by 15M barrels since the end of May, dragging inventories right back to normal operating levels.

And float of available Iranian ballast tankers sitting in the Persian Gulf might buy them a bit of a tactical window. We're talking 24 Iranian vessels parked out there right now.

But let’s be real—this is just a temporary band-aid. While Iranian crude supply staged a solid recovery post-MoU, a renewed blockade means domestic production is ultimately bound to point down.

The only real saving grace for Tehran is they spent the last month aggressively shoving as many hulls down the pipe to Asia as humanly possible. 

Between the physical voyage days and the lag on settlements and wire clearances, their FX revenue safety net won't completely hit a brick wall for another 60-90 days.

#oott #iran

I’m still sitting here thinking this whole setup isn’t nearly enough to flip everything.

Look, oil mkt was heavily tilted short, and the street was already pricing in a technical dead-cat bounce, so this violent short squeeze isn't exactly catching anyone off guard.

I’m not betting the house that Trump drops a TACO print automatically. The ball is 100% in Tehran's court. With the US officially shouting out a maritime blockade refresh, Iran has to punch back. Sitting on their hands right now means lighting whatever leverage they have left on fire.

If Iranian barrels get choked out, the textbook playbook for them is to hold their leverage hostage by capping everyone else’s inbound and outbound lanes. Where their pain threshold actually sits, though, is anyone's guess. But let’s be real—flimsy headline blockades, symbolic drone strikes on US outposts, and minor tanker skirmishes aren't going to move the needle anymore.

If Iran actually goes hot and starts hammering GCC infrastructure or executes a bulletproof, total lockdown of the Red Sea—bc let’s face it, that hasn't cleanly hit the tape yet—that’s your structural runway to macro escalation. If they push it that far, even Trump will eventually have to map out an exit strategy.

You guys can talk about the math all day, but trust me, I run the same models. The only reason my PnL took a hit in that final month was because that exact math completely flatlined on me.

Strictly speaking, if a hull catches fire and Hormuz goes dark again, the tape should be screaming $150 oil. But the street has massive scar tissue from watching the market effortlessly fade geo-headlines in the past, so the money is naturally going to gravitate right back toward that short-bias default.

We need a smoking gun here—an absolute tail risk print that no desk can handwave away. Either Iran puts real kinetic heat on regional infrastructure to cause undeniable physical damage, or the US-Tehran back-and-forth morphs into total, unhedged warfare.

Without that kind of tail-event acceleration, keeping crude pegged at these highs is going to be a massive uphill battle. Bottom line, looking at the tape over the last few months, the current catalyst mix just isn't getting the job done. Though to be fair, we’ve got two massive wildcards sitting out there—one bull, one bear.

The bull case: China. Beijing knows the West’s strategic buffer is completely running on fumes. They could easily weaponize the tape, push crude into a massive squeeze, and put the squeeze on the US administration. Granted they left that trade on the table last time, but you can’t cross it out.

The bear case: Trump's TACO. The exact monster everyone in this room is staring at. Let’s be real—Trump could jump on social media right now, print a single line saying 'Not everyone has to pay,' and you'd instantly see $4-5 washed out of crude.

When the dust settles on this squeeze, macro funds are going to need a bulletproof thesis to stay long the front of the curve. Everyone on the street still has PTSD from the consecutive VaR shocks and the TACO traps we just went through. Holding paper longs here takes a serious amount of delta-courage.

We need a total smoking gun here. It might sound completely unhinged, and ppl can point fingers at me all they want, but let's be real—haven’t we all taken enough pain over the last few months to learn our lesson?

Fool me once, shame on you. Fool me twice, shame on me. Fool me three times, and you're officially a co-conspirator.

Let's watch the tape play out.

While the traditionally inverse relationship between oil and gold appears to be softening, it is still too early to conclude that the correlation has broken down. It may, however, signal that the market is becoming increasingly concerned about the broader economic fallout from a sustained energy shock. Tightness in refined fuel markets has already pushed diesel and gasoline prices to levels consistent with Brent trading well above USD 100 per barrel, raising the risk that growth concerns may increasingly offset inflation and rate-related headwinds for gold.

Brent just surged nearly 13% in two sessions to $86. The Iran ceasefire that markets spent three weeks pricing in has effectively broken down.
US strikes on Iranian military sites tied to Hormuz attacks, combined with a reinstated blockade on Iranian vessels, have repriced energy risk overnight. Exxon climbed 4.1% on the news. Nvidia fell 3.5% as tech absorbed the broader risk-off move.
Here’s the collision that matters today specifically: CPI prints this morning, Federal Reserve Chair Warsh testifies before Congress, and JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Goldman Sachs all report earnings — all on the same day that oil just moved 13% in 48 hours.
Each of those four events would move markets independently. Together they create a stress test for whether the current rally in risk assets was pricing a world where energy inflation was fading. It wasn’t fading. It paused.
The CLARITY Act revised version is also expected in Congress next week — crypto regulation’s most important legislative moment in years arriving into the same week as an energy shock and bank earnings.
Markets rarely give you this many binary events simultaneously. The question isn’t which one matters most. It’s whether portfolios built for one scenario can absorb all four at once.

Just in: Is the United States experiencing "negative CPI" on a month-on-month basis for the first time in six years? But that's not the point tonight.

Tonight, the attention of the global market will once again focus on a crucial US economic data - the US Bureau of Labor Statistics will release the June CPI report at 20:30 Beijing time.

As the interest rate market estimates that the probability of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates in July is close to 50%, this report is likely to determine the direction of the Federal Reserve's policy.

According to economists surveyed by the media, it is generally predicted that the drop in gasoline prices is expected to turn the month-on-month CPI increase into negative value in June, which will be the first time in six years. However, this may not mean that the inflation crisis is over.

Excluding volatile food and energy prices, year-over-year core inflation is expected to remain well above the Fed's target. U.S. CPI change chart (final value): The month-on-month increase was negative last time, and it dates back to the 2020 COVID-19 epidemic.

Some industry insiders pointed out that the good news tonight is that inflationary pressures have at least temporarily stabilized in June, and the surge in oil prices in early 2026 does not appear to have spread to other parts of the economy except for the most energy-sensitive sectors.

But the more critical factor now may be how other variables in the inflation picture will change... JP Morgan chief economist Bruce Kasman wrote that June's CPI data should confirm the good news on overall inflation: the surge in energy prices that pushed overall CPI to more than 4% in May is now reversing. However, core inflation may send a mixed message.

Due to the recent strong performance of the labor market, the market generally believes that the Fed is currently more concerned about stubborn inflation than the labor market. Industry analysts also generally say that CPI data is currently more critical to the Fed's policymaking than non-farm payrolls.

The bond market is currently pricing in one to two rate hikes by the Federal Reserve before the end of the year. With the renewed conflict in the Strait of Hormuz causing oil prices to rise again, some analysts believe that the probability of raising interest rates is further increasing.

The following is the latest estimate of tonight's CPI data from industry organizations: CPI in June is expected to increase by 3.8% year-on-year, compared with an increase of 4.2% in May. The CPI in June is expected to decrease by 0.1% month-on-month, compared with an increase of 0.5% in May.

Core CPI is expected to increase by 2.8% year-on-year in June, compared with an increase of 2.9% in May. Core CPI is expected to increase by 0.2% month-on-month in June, compared with an increase of 0.2% in May.

As shown in the figure below, the median estimate of some institutions calculated by Nick Timirao of "New Federal Reserve News Agency" even tends to believe that the month-on-month decline in CPI in June will reach -0.2%.

Gasoline prices will drag down overall CPI in June There is no doubt that the main driver of overall CPI in June is expected to be the decline in gasoline prices, which stemmed from the collapse in oil prices after the memorandum to end the war with Iran was reached. UBS economists expect gasoline and energy prices to fall more than 9% for the month.

According to data from the American Automobile Association, since peaking on May 20, gasoline prices across the United States have fallen by 76 cents per gallon, a decrease of 17%. This factor would push June's CPI back by about 0.42 percentage points, they wrote.

Overall, "the breadth of inflationary pressures is close to historical norms," wrote Christopher Hodge, chief economist at Natixis.

"The energy shock from the Iran war has not been broadly transmitted to core commodity prices, and while there may still be some transmission pressures, we view these as both weak and short-lived." Hodge himself predicts that the overall CPI will fall by 0.07% month-on-month in June, and the year-on-year increase will fall back to 3.90%.

He expects core CPI to rise 0.24% month-on-month and 2.80% year-on-year.

Economists at Deutsche Bank are focusing on the pass-through effect of lower energy prices: "We expect price stickiness on the downside to lead to relatively modest declines in air tickets and express fees, but if lower oil prices are passed through to consumers more quickly, these categories may face more significant downside risks." Of course, investors need to be reminded that the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding that led to the sharp drop in U.S.

gasoline prices in June was on the verge of collapse last week. Since then, the United States’ new round of air strikes against Iran over the weekend and Iran’s proposal to block the Strait of Hormuz have pushed up oil prices.

Although the fall in energy prices in June eased the pressure on the overall CPI data, it only reflects a microcosm of past price levels, and the oil price levels at that time may no longer be applicable now. July's CPI data - which will be released in August - will likely still differ significantly from tonight's data.

Investors who interpret weak energy market data in June as signs of cooling inflation and imminent interest rate cuts could be making a major mistake. The Fed itself is expected to remain focused on what's next for energy markets. The highlight of tonight will be Core Data?

Therefore, given that the current fluctuations in the energy market are still highly uncertain, the focus of tonight's US June CPI data may still be on changes in core data.

JPMorgan's Kasman said that while energy's transmission effect on core inflation has been limited so far, service sector inflation remains high and core inflation appears to be sticky at around 3%.

Even though core inflation peaked earlier than expected, indicators in the survey - including Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) prices, delivery times, supply chain indicators and transportation costs - still point to increasing pressure on the supply of core commodities. Economists say one new item to watch is electronics prices.

Later in June, Apple was forced to raise prices on MacBooks and iPads. Although this occurred at the end of the month and may not have been captured in time by June's CPI data, it still reflects the upward price pressure that the AI infrastructure boom has brought to the entire hardware industry.

According to the Federal Reserve's June minutes, the boom in AI investment has been listed as one of the three forces pushing up inflation, along with wars in the Middle East and tariffs. These forces may keep prices high and prompt the Federal Reserve to turn to raising interest rates.

Goldman Sachs economist Megan Peters calculated in the latest report that the triple effects of AI-driven memory price surges, software price increases, and electricity price increases have now increased the U.S.

core PCE year-on-year inflation rate by more than 0.2 percentage points, and this contribution is expected to rise to 0.5 percentage points by the end of the year. In addition, the impact of the tariffs imposed by the Trump administration is expected to continue to be reflected in the latest CPI data.

Jeff Roach, chief economist at LPL, said high import costs and energy costs are "confounding market expectations" and are causing higher labor and service costs in the health care sector in particular. "When we think about doctor visits, dentist visits and eyeglasses, some of these commodity prices are coming into play.

As the cost of importing these products increases, the prices of these professional services are starting to trend upward." In its latest CPI forecast, Goldman Sachs predicts that core CPI will increase by 0.17% month-on-month in June, with a year-on-year increase of +2.76%.

Goldman Sachs highlighted four key sub-trends expected in the report: soft auto inflation is expected; a mild inflation reading in the housing category; a modest rise in the travel services category; and potential residual seasonality that will bring downward pressure.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that it expects core CPI month-on-month growth to hover around 0.2% in the next few months, reflecting the continued slowdown in inflation in the housing category, as well as the easing of upward pressure on the travel services category from rising jet fuel prices and the World Cup; however, if oil market turmoil and related oil price increases are more prolonged than expected, risks will be tilted to the upside.

Will tonight's data determine the direction of the Fed's decision this month? It is worth noting that the CPI data release comes just hours before Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh’s semi-annual congressional testimony.

The performance of inflation data may not only affect the Federal Reserve’s decision-making in the July interest rate resolution, but may also directly determine the market’s pricing logic for the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy in the second half of the year.

Pricing in the interest rate swap market showed that traders on Monday had significantly increased their bets on the Federal Reserve raising interest rates by 25 basis points in July after the United States launched a series of new strikes on Iran over the weekend.

The latest pricing shows that the probability of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates by the end of this month is close to 50%, up from less than 40% earlier on Monday.

At the same time, the probability that the Federal Reserve will raise interest rates at least twice before the end of this year has jumped to 56% from 34% at the beginning of this month. This also means two interest rate hikes - becoming the most likely interest rate change scenario for the Federal Reserve this year.

Regarding tonight's CPI data, Fed Governor Waller said on Monday that if the upcoming data shows that inflation continues to be well above the 2% target, the Fed may need to raise interest rates "in the near future." Waller bluntly stated that the Fed's next move will depend on this week's inflation data - if the data moves in an adverse direction, the Fed

should not "take it lightly." Economists at Deutsche Bank said "mixed" economic progress since the last Fed meeting could prompt some policymakers to take a more hawkish stance. But LPL’s Roach doesn’t think the Fed will raise interest rates anytime soon. "I think the discussion about maintaining a hawkish bias will continue," he said.

"I don't think they will raise rates in July, and if inflation improves in the next few months, it is possible that they will keep rates unchanged in the next few meetings." The Fed is currently weighing a number of economic indicators to set the tone for policy for the rest of the year.

Judging from the schedule, after the release of CPI data, another major financial event tonight will undoubtedly be Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh’s trip to Capitol Hill at 22:00 Beijing time.

Federal Reserve observers will pay close attention to his views on inflation, the labor market and economic growth - and the interaction between these factors and interest rates. (

Street Signal | Gulf oil export uncertainty exists in the short term, long-term pipelines can be hedged (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs commented on oil exports from the Gulf region, believing that short-term uncertainty has increased, but in the long term, diversified pipeline export capabilities can serve as an effective hedging tool.

The report pointed out that the market is worried about potential supply disruption risks in the Strait of Hormuz, and this concern has pushed up oil price volatility.

However, alternative pipelines that have been commissioned by major oil producers such as Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, as well as the spare capacity they maintain, can support most exports without relying on the strait.

This suggests that the actual risk of disruption to the crude oil supply chain is exaggerated and that the market may be mispricing the short-term risk premium.

One-sentence conclusion: Short-term geopolitical risks have pushed up oil prices, but the Gulf countries' strong export pipeline network and spare capacity are effective hedging tools, and long-term oil prices lack a basis for continued rise. Positive/negative: Negative for crude oil prices, short-term risk premiums may fall.

The current market pricing of geo-risk is partially correct, but Goldman Sachs believes the risk is overestimated and long-term prices do not fully reflect true supply potential. Catalysts:

1) Any actual military conflict or blockade in the Strait of Hormuz;

2) OPEC+ production decision-making meeting;

3) Saudi or UAE pipeline export flow data.

## US Stock Market Summary: Monday, July 13
The three major US stock indices fell across the board, led by tech stocks—particularly the semiconductor sector—as markets were dragged down by inflation concerns triggered by escalating US-Iran geopolitical tensions and surging oil prices. The Nasdaq Composite suffered the sharpest decline, snapping its previous three-day winning streak.
### Closing Performance of Major Indices
 * **Dow Jones Industrial Average**: Closed at **52,498.64**, down 138.37 points (-0.26%).
 * **S&P 500 Index**: Closed at **7,515.34**, down approximately 0.79%.
 * **Nasdaq Composite Index**: Closed at **25,873.18**, down approximately 1.55%.
### Key Market Drivers
 * **Escalating US-Iran Geopolitical Tensions**
   Over the weekend, both sides exchanged airstrikes. The US took new actions near the Strait of Hormuz, while Iran retaliated against US allies. Donald Trump announced that "the ceasefire is over" and reinstated a shipping blockade on Iranian vessels through the Strait of Hormuz (including a 20% transit fee), sparking severe market anxiety over the security of critical energy corridors.
 * **Surging Oil Prices**
   Brent crude briefly spiked above $83 per barrel, and WTI crude rose past $78, marking significant single-day gains that approached double digits at certain points. The jump in oil prices fueled inflation expectations, prompting the market to recalibrate its outlook on the probability of Federal Reserve rate cuts.
 * **Collective Pullback in Semiconductor and Tech Stocks**
   South Korean chip giant SK Hynix plummeted around 9% to 15% on its first day of trading following its US listing, marking a record single-day drop and dragging down the entire US chip sector. Major semiconductor stocks like Intel, Micron, AMD, and ARM generally slid by over 4% to 6%, putting the heaviest pressure on the Nasdaq.
### Other Market Highlights
 * **Bank Stocks**: Ahead of the official kickoff to the bank earnings season, major financial institutions like JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs experienced pullbacks.
 * **Other Sectors**: Supported by rising oil prices, the energy sector showed relative resilience. Conversely, several high-growth tech stocks (e.g., Shopify, AppLovin) fell in tandem with the broader market.
 * **Macro Backdrop**: Investors are closely watching Tuesday's (July 14) June CPI inflation data and the forthcoming banking corporate earnings. While S&P 500 Q2 earnings are still projected to grow strongly at around 20.9% year-over-year, surging oil prices and geopolitical risks have introduced short-term uncertainty.
### Overall Evaluation
> The US stock market exhibited a clear **"risk-off"** tone on July 13, with geopolitics and energy prices taking center stage, prompting profit-taking across the tech and AI sectors. While the overall losses remain contained, short-term market volatility is likely to intensify.

美股7月13日（周一）总结

美股三大指数全线下挫，科技股尤其是半导体板块领跌，受美伊地缘政治紧张和油价暴涨引发的通胀担忧拖累。纳斯达克指数跌幅最大，结束了此前三连涨。

主要指数收盘表现
道琼斯工业平均指数 (Dow Jones)：收于 **52,498.64 点，下跌 138.37 点（-0.26%）。
标普500指数 (S&P 500)：收于 **7,515.34 点，下跌约 0.79%。
纳斯达克综合指数 (Nasdaq Composite)：收于 **25,873.18 点，下跌约 1.55%。

主要驱动因素
美伊地缘政治紧张升级  
   周末美伊双方互有空袭，美国在霍尔木兹海峡附近采取新行动，伊朗则对美盟友进行报复。特朗普宣布“停火已结束”，并重启对伊朗通过霍尔木兹海峡的航运封锁（含20%过境费），引发市场对能源通道安全的担忧。

油价大幅上涨  
   布伦特原油一度飙升至83美元/桶以上，WTI原油升至78美元以上，单日涨幅显著（部分时段接近两位数）。油价上涨加剧通胀预期，市场对美联储降息的概率出现调整。

半导体与科技股集体回调  
   韩国芯片巨头 SK Hynix 在首日美股上市后暴跌约9-15%（创纪录最大单日跌幅），拖累美股芯片板块。英特尔（Intel）、美光（Micron）、AMD、ARM等芯片股普遍下跌4%-6%以上。纳斯达克因此承压最大。

其他市场亮点
银行股**：银行财报季即将开启，多家大行（如摩根大通、高盛等）出现回调。
其他板块**：能源板块因油价上涨相对抗跌；部分科技成长股（如Shopify、AppLovin）也随大盘走弱。
宏观背景**：市场正等待周二（7月14日）的6月CPI通胀数据，以及即将公布的银行财报。标普500二季度盈利预期仍较高（同比增约20.9%），但油价和地缘风险带来短期不确定性。

总体评价：7月13日美股呈现“风险厌恶”行情，地缘政治与能源价格成为主导因素，科技/AI板块出现获利了结。整体跌幅尚属可控，但短期波动性可能加大。

Just in: Many central banks will discuss interest rates for the first time in the second half of the year or send a tight signal.

This week, many central banks will hold their first interest rate meeting in the second half of the year. Among them, there are expectations of interest rate hikes in South Korea, which affects global market sentiment and may further strengthen the overall trend of global monetary policy tightening.

Industry insiders analyze that the current economic recovery foundation of most economies is not yet solid, and multiple factors are adding up to push up the stickiness of inflation. Central banks of various countries are in a dilemma when it comes to policy decisions.

South Korea may join the interest rate hike camp This week, South Korea, Canada, and Switzerland will release the minutes of their latest monetary policy meetings. South Korea is expected to follow Europe, Japan, and New Zealand as an economy that enters the interest rate hike camp. The Bank of Korea will hold a monetary policy meeting on July 16.

Bank of Korea Governor Shin Hyun-song recently publicly stated that the current strong growth of emerging industries continues to support the economy and it is necessary to tighten monetary policy.

He said it is necessary to raise interest rates "at an appropriate time in the future" and that the timing of raising interest rates will depend on the degree of inflationary pressures, the pace of economic recovery and financial stability.

Shin Hyun-song expects that the enhanced demand brought about by the economic recovery will offset the downward pressure of falling global oil prices, and consumer inflation will remain above the target level. Citibank predicts that the Bank of Korea will raise its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points to 2.75% at this meeting.

Citi economists said in a latest report that Shin Hyun-song may hint in a post-meeting statement or press conference that a gradual increase in interest rates will be adopted in the future to balance the relationship between inflation control and economic growth.

The report predicts that the Bank of Korea will maintain the frequency of raising interest rates by 25 basis points every quarter in the second half of 2026, that is, once in July and October; it may raise interest rates again in January and April 2027.

Bloomberg quoted experts as saying that rising inflation in South Korea, economic expansion driven by the semiconductor industry, and upward risks to the property market have pushed the Bank of Korea's policy stance to the most hawkish level in many years. The Bank of Korea will also weigh whether chip-driven growth is limited to a few industries.

Currently, household debt in South Korea continues to rise, and housing prices in core cities continue to rise. Officials increasingly advocate that monetary policy needs to work in tandem with macro-prudential policies to prevent financial imbalances while avoiding dampening investment vitality in the field of artificial intelligence.

The Bank of Canada and the Swiss National Bank are likely to keep interest rates unchanged this week. The resilience and expected improvement in the economies of the two countries increase the possibility of a shift in monetary policy towards a tighter orientation.

Bloomberg quoted market analysts as saying that the prospects for the implementation of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement in the second half of the year will gradually become clearer, which will boost domestic investment and employment in Canada and create favorable conditions for the Bank of Canada to raise interest rates by 25 basis points to 2.5% at the end of the year.

The Swiss National Bank, which has maintained zero interest rates for more than a year, recently stated that rising energy prices have also pushed up Swiss inflation, and that other factors have limited impact.

However, at this stage, the Swiss National Bank is focusing its policy on foreign exchange intervention to deal with the pressure of excessive appreciation of the Swiss franc caused by geopolitical turmoil.

Europe and the United States welcome more tightening motivations Affected by high energy prices and rising prices of upstream raw materials driven by the AI industry chain, inflation remains sticky in Europe and the United States, which has become an important reason for the tightening of monetary policy.

The Federal Reserve recently released new chairman Kevin Warsh's first semi-annual monetary policy report on its official website. The report shows that U.S. inflation has further increased this spring, driven by factors such as the continued impact of U.S.

tariff policies, rising energy prices due to conflicts in the Middle East, and surge in demand driven by AI infrastructure construction. The report points out that AI infrastructure construction continues to have strong demand for electricity, chips and other materials, creating price pressure in the near future.

Peters, an economist at Goldman Sachs, calculated in the latest report that the triple effects of AI-driven memory price surges, software price increases, and electricity price increases have now increased the U.S.

core PCE year-on-year inflation rate by more than 0.2 percentage points, and the figure is expected to rise to 0.5 percentage points by the end of the year. The report reiterated the Fed's commitment to maintaining price stability.

In its report, the Fed proposed "another way of looking at inflation." By excluding "some of the effects of abnormal price movements," the censored average annual PCE inflation rate compiled by the Dallas Fed fell to 2.4% in May from 2.6% last May.

According to the schedule, Warsh will attend House of Representatives and Senate committee hearings respectively on the 14th and 15th local time to accept a routine mid-year review of monetary policy. In addition, the Federal Reserve also announced the recent establishment of five independent working groups.

The leaders of the working groups include well-known scholars, former central bank officials and corporate executives. The working group will conduct assessments around monetary policy, balance sheet policy, economic data, productivity and employment, and inflation frameworks.

Analysts said that Warsh's extensive introduction of external professionals to relevant working groups shows that this round of adjustments by the Fed not only focuses on the monetary policy formulation mechanism, but also focuses on issues such as artificial intelligence, productivity improvement, and data quality, highlighting the complexity of current policy formulation.

The complexity of European policy arises from the risk of energy-induced inflation. The European Central Bank recently released the minutes of its June interest rate decision meeting, which showed that all members agreed that the risk of upward inflation in the euro area has increased significantly.

The conflict in the Middle East has pushed up energy costs and lengthened the cycle of high prices. The market's prediction that the European Central Bank will raise interest rates three times throughout the year was also confirmed by discussions among officials attending the meeting.

At the interest rate meeting in June, the European Central Bank raised the deposit rate by 25 basis points, the first rate increase in the past three years. Minute data showed that the Eurozone's adjusted CPI rose to 3.2% in May, core inflation rose to 2.5%, and service industry inflation reached 3.5%.

Energy price increases have gradually been transmitted to the food, industrial products and service fields. Even if calculated according to the pace of this round of interest rate hikes, overall inflation will continue to be higher than the 2% policy target before the first half of 2027. Officials focused on potential risks in energy.

If crude oil and natural gas prices fail to fall as expected in futures, inflationary stickiness will further intensify.

Although the duration of this round of energy shocks is expected to be shorter than the 2022 energy crisis, the combination of global trade frictions, rising wages, and price increases in the service industry will increase the probability of another rebound in inflation.

Middle East conflict boosts expectations for tightening monetary policy Bloomberg Economics analyzed the policy forecasts of 23 central banks around the world and pointed out that after the United States launched military strikes in the Middle East, the interest rate path of global central banks has now been raised compared with pre-war expectations, and this high level will remain for many years.

Borrowing cost forecasts compiled by the agency show that the interest rate trajectory until 2028 will be as much as 0.5% or more higher than pre-war assumptions, whether measured by a composite indicator of global interest rates or an indicator of advanced economies.

The expectation that global monetary policy will be tight stems from the continued evolution of inflation risks: on the one hand, the implementation of artificial intelligence will bring periodic inflation pressure; on the other hand, there is uncertainty about the navigation of the Strait of Hormuz, and energy disturbances continue to push up commodity prices.

Even if the conflict in the Middle East eases, the cost pressure caused by the war will still be transmitted to businesses and residents for a period of time, superimposed on the high interest rate environment, giving rise to previously unforeseen economic pressure.

Earlier this year, Bloomberg Economics predicted that the Fed would cut interest rates by a cumulative 1 percentage point by mid-2027, but now only one cut of 25 basis points is expected.

The European Central Bank is expected to raise interest rates again to a level half a percentage point higher than originally planned, and then gradually lower interest rates.

Jamie Rush, global head of economics at Bloomberg Economics, concluded, “After experiencing the inflationary shock during the epidemic period, central banks have generally taken a tough stance on inflation.

In the face of another surge in price increases, even if it is only a short-lived phenomenon, the willingness to withdraw hawkish rhetoric appears to be limited.

Even if oil prices fall, central bank speech indicators generally remain in the hawkish range.” However, Bloomberg Economics' outlook also shows that global economies are more resilient than expected to withstand high interest rates and have the ability to withstand successive external shocks. (

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 US–IRAN FIGHTING PUTS HORMUZ BACK AT THE CENTRE

The US and Iran exchanged five major waves of attacks over the weekend and into Monday morning, as their struggle for control of the Strait of Hormuz intensified.

The US said it targeted Iranian:

🔸 air-defence systems;

🔸 coastal radar sites;

🔸 missile and drone capabilities;

🔸 military boats operating around the strait.

Iran responded by claiming attacks on US-linked military facilities in Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman and Jordan.

Tehran says the Strait of Hormuz is closed.

Washington disputes that claim and says escorted vessels are still passing.

The most accurate conclusion is that the strait is severely disrupted, but not completely sealed.

Commercial traffic has slowed dramatically as shipping companies avoid exposing vessels and crews to missiles, drones and attacks at sea.

Simple explanation:

The Strait of Hormuz is like a narrow doorway used by a huge part of the world’s energy supply.

The doorway does not need to be physically locked to cause a crisis.

If ships consider it too dangerous to pass, the practical result can be almost the same.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 OIL RISES AS ENERGY-SUPPLY FEARS RETURN

Brent crude climbed approximately 4% towards $79 a barrel, while US WTI crude rose towards $74.

Oil remains below the extreme levels reached earlier in the war, but the renewed increase matters because markets had started expecting shipping conditions to improve.

Those expectations are now being reversed.

Before the conflict, approximately one-fifth of global oil supplies passed through Hormuz.

That makes disruption around the strait a direct threat to global energy availability, inflation and economic growth.

No LNG carriers were reported to have transited Hormuz since Saturday, while European natural-gas prices also rose.

Europe is particularly exposed because its jet-fuel stocks provide less than one month of demand cover.

Britain, France and Germany could face particular pressure if Middle Eastern supplies are interrupted for a prolonged period.

Simple explanation:

Higher oil does not remain confined to petrol stations.

It raises the cost of flying, transporting goods, manufacturing products and delivering food.

Eventually, those costs can reach consumers through higher prices.

That means even good news can trigger selling because investors decide the price has moved too far ahead of reality.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE AI TRADE IS BEING REPRICED—NOT DISPROVED

The semiconductor sell-off does not mean that demand for AI infrastructure has disappeared.

TSMC reported record second-quarter revenue of approximately T$1.27tn—or $39.6bn—representing growth of 36% from a year earlier.

The figure slightly exceeded market expectations.

June revenue alone rose almost 68% year on year.

TSMC manufactures advanced processors for companies including Nvidia and Apple, making its performance one of the strongest indicators of real semiconductor demand.

The company will report full second-quarter earnings and provide its latest outlook on Thursday.

That guidance could become one of the week’s most important tests for the entire AI trade.

Simple explanation:

Two things can be true simultaneously:

🔸 the AI industry can continue growing rapidly;

🔸 AI-related share prices can still fall because investors previously paid too much.

Strong businesses do not always equal attractive shares at every price.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 TREASURY YIELDS RISE ACROSS THE CURVE

US Treasury yields moved higher:

🔸 1-year yield: 4.073%, up 1.3 basis points

🔸 2-year yield: 4.221%, up 0.9 basis point

🔸 10-year yield: 4.575%, up 1.4 basis points

🔸 30-year yield: 5.077%, up 1.8 basis points

The 30-year yield remains above 5%, keeping pressure on expensive technology shares and other assets valued heavily on distant future profits.

Rising short-term yields suggest markets remain concerned about Federal Reserve policy.

Rising long-term yields suggest investors are also worried about persistent inflation, heavy government borrowing and the longer-term cost of capital.

Simple explanation:

When investors fear that inflation may remain high, they demand a greater return for lending money.

That pushes bond yields upwards.

Higher yields also make bonds more competitive with shares, particularly highly valued technology companies.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 GOLD FALLS DESPITE THE GEOPOLITICAL CRISIS

Gold fell more than 1% towards $4,066 an ounce.

That may appear surprising because gold is often considered a safe-haven asset.

However, Treasury yields are rising, while gold pays no interest.

The Dollar Index is actually slightly lower at approximately 100.88, meaning today’s pressure on gold appears to be coming more from rising yields than from dollar strength.

Simple explanation:

Investors may prefer interest-paying government bonds when yields rise, even during geopolitical uncertainty.

Gold can therefore fall despite increased fear.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BITCOIN TRADES LIKE A RISK ASSET

Bitcoin fell approximately 2% towards $62,700–$62,900 as investors reduced exposure to volatile assets.

Bitcoin is sometimes described as digital gold.

But during sudden market shocks, it frequently behaves more like a highly volatile technology investment.

Simple explanation:

When investors urgently want safety or cash, they often sell the assets that move most dramatically first.

Today, Bitcoin is behaving more like a risk asset than a safe haven.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 FED SPEAKERS COME BEFORE TOMORROW’S MAJOR TEST

Fed governors Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman are due to speak today.

Investors will be listening for clues about whether renewed oil inflation is changing their view of interest rates.

Waller’s comments may be particularly important.

Markets will want to know whether he still considers monetary policy sufficiently restrictive now that:

🔸 inflation has remained stubborn;

🔸 oil prices are rising again;

🔸 Treasury yields are moving higher;

🔸 economic growth remains resilient.

Tomorrow, Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will present the Federal Reserve’s semi-annual monetary-policy report to Congress.

His testimony comes on the same day as the June US consumer-price report.

That combination could produce major moves in bonds, currencies and technology shares.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 INFLATION DATA COULD DETERMINE THE NEXT MARKET MOVE

🔸Tuesday brings the June consumer-price index.

🔸Wednesday brings producer-price inflation.

Investors will be looking for evidence that higher energy costs are spreading into the wider economy.

Simple explanation:

Consumer inflation measures what households pay.

Producer inflation measures the costs faced by businesses.

If business costs rise, companies may eventually pass those increases on to customers.

A hotter-than-expected inflation report would increase the risk that the Federal Reserve keeps rates high—or raises them further.

A softer reading could calm bond markets, although another sustained oil-price surge would still complicate the outlook.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BANK EARNINGS WILL TEST WALL STREET OPTIMISM

Earnings season accelerates tomorrow with results from:

🔸 JPMorgan Chase;

🔸 Goldman Sachs;

🔸 Bank of America;

🔸 Wells Fargo;

🔸 Citigroup.

The five largest US investment banks are expected to generate approximately $11.1bn in second-quarter investment-banking fees—around 27% more than a year earlier.

Equity-capital-market fees alone are forecast to reach approximately $2.5bn, potentially the strongest result since 2021.

IPOs, share sales and mergers have revived, generating valuable fees for Wall Street.

Simple explanation:

Investment banks make money when companies:

🔸 list shares;

🔸 raise capital;

🔸 buy other businesses;

🔸 sell divisions;

🔸 restructure debt.

More corporate deals mean more fees.

But expectations are already high.

Strong results may not be enough unless bank executives also provide confident guidance about lending, consumer health, trading activity and future dealmaking.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 CHINA’S ECONOMY FACES ANOTHER GROWTH TEST

China’s economy is expected to have grown approximately 4.5% from a year earlier during the second quarter, slowing from 5% in the first quarter.

Exports remain relatively resilient, partly helped by AI-related demand.

But domestic consumption, private investment and the property market remain weak.

China’s second-quarter GDP, retail-sales, industrial-production and investment data are due on Wednesday.

Simple explanation:

China’s factories are still selling substantial quantities of goods abroad.

But Chinese households and private companies remain cautious about spending at home.

That makes economic growth increasingly dependent on exports and government support.

Investors will be watching for signs that Beijing may introduce additional fiscal stimulus later this year.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE US LABOUR MARKET LOOKS STRONGER THAN IT FEELS

The unemployment rate remains low, but hiring has slowed sharply.

Economists increasingly describe the current environment as “low-hire, low-fire”:

🔸 companies are not dismissing many existing workers;

🔸 but they are also reluctant to create new positions;

🔸 unemployed people and graduates are struggling to get hired;

🔸 fewer workers are confident enough to leave their jobs.

Simple explanation:

The unemployment rate tells us that relatively few people are being fired.

It does not tell us that finding a new job is easy.

The labour market may be relatively safe for people already employed, but extremely difficult for those trying to enter it.

This matters because even employed consumers may spend less when they fear that replacing a lost job would be difficult.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHAT TO WATCH THIS WEEK

💠 Tuesday

🔸 June US consumer inflation

🔸 China trade data

🔸 JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, Wells Fargo and Citigroup earnings

🔸 Fed Chair Kevin Warsh testifies before the House Financial Services Committee

💠 Wednesday

🔸 June US producer inflation

🔸 China’s second-quarter GDP and activity data

🔸 Federal Reserve Beige Book

🔸 Morgan Stanley, BlackRock and ASML earnings

🔸 Bank of Canada interest-rate decision

💠 Thursday

🔸 US retail sales

🔸 weekly jobless claims

🔸 TSMC, Netflix, UnitedHealth and United Airlines earnings

💠 Friday

🔸 US consumer sentiment

🔸 housing starts

🔸 industrial production

🔸 China’s World AI Conference begins in Shanghai

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS

Today’s market is sending seven important messages.

1️⃣ Hormuz remains the world’s most important immediate market risk

The crisis matters because it threatens one of the world’s most important energy arteries.

Even partial disruption can lift oil, natural gas, shipping and insurance costs.

2️⃣ Oil has become an inflation trade again

A sustained rise would reduce the Federal Reserve’s freedom to cut rates and could increase the risk of further tightening.

3️⃣ The AI trade is being repriced, not disproved

TSMC’s record revenue shows that demand remains powerful.

SK Hynix’s fall shows what can happen when strong fundamentals meet extreme valuations, leverage and crowded positioning.

4️⃣ The market is showing stress, not panic

The Nasdaq is falling much more than the Dow, while the VIX remains near 16.

Investors are reducing exposure selectively rather than selling every asset indiscriminately.

5️⃣ Long-term bond yields remain a major threat

The 30-year Treasury yield above 5% increases financing costs and places pressure on companies whose valuations depend heavily on future growth.

6️⃣ Inflation and earnings now matter even more

Oil has already revived inflation anxiety.

This week’s CPI, PPI, bank earnings and TSMC outlook will reveal whether economic and corporate fundamentals can withstand higher borrowing costs.

7️⃣ Global growth is becoming increasingly uneven

🔸 The US labour market has low layoffs but weak hiring.

🔸China has resilient exports but weak domestic demand.

🔸AI investment remains strong, while consumers and traditional industries face greater pressure.

This is not a simple strong or weak economy.

It is an increasingly divided one.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BOTTOM LINE

The market entered Monday facing two very different challenges.

The US–Iran conflict threatens the flow of energy through the global economy.

The semiconductor sell-off challenges the valuations supporting global equity markets.

Oil is rising.

Treasury yields are rising.

The Nasdaq is falling.

Volatility is increasing.

Bitcoin is weakening.

Yet TSMC’s record revenue shows that the underlying AI-investment cycle remains strong.

This is not a straightforward collapse in fundamentals.

It is a market confronting excessive expectations, crowded positioning, geopolitical instability and renewed inflation risk—all at the same time.

The next major direction will probably be determined by four forces:

🔸 whether Hormuz traffic deteriorates or recovers;

🔸 whether inflation surprises higher;

🔸 what Kevin Warsh says about interest rates;

🔸 whether bank and semiconductor earnings justify current valuations.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💭 THOUGHT FOR THE DAY

“When oil rises, yields rise and crowded trades unwind, the market is not changing the story—it is changing the price of risk.”

💰 What would a 20% toll on Strait of Hormuz cargo actually be worth?

The headline math: petroleum cargo alone transiting the Strait is worth $500-580 billion annually. A flat 20% cut of that would be $100-116 billion a year just from oil. Add LNG and refined products (total energy trade through the strait can exceed $1 trillion/year), and a 20% toll on everything could theoretically top $200 billion annually.

But here's why that number is mostly fantasy, per maritime/legal analysts:

It's not legal under international law. The Strait of Hormuz is a natural waterway, not an engineered one like the Suez Canal (which can legally charge fees because it requires constant dredging/maintenance). Under UNCLOS's "transit passage" regime, states are prohibited from charging vessels simply for passing through — a rule treated as customary international law even by countries that haven't ratified the treaty. The UN's own shipping agency has already publicly opposed fees for any strait.

Realistic fee levels are far smaller. Using actual Suez-style pricing (~$535,000 per large tanker) applied to Hormuz's roughly 10 large crude carriers/day, one analysis puts a realistic annual toll figure closer to $1.5 billion — not $100B+.

The countries paying would push back hard. China, India, Japan, and South Korea take the overwhelming majority of oil through the strait — major economies with real leverage, unlikely to simply absorb a unilateral 20% tax without retaliation, rerouting, or legal challenge.

It would shrink the very traffic being taxed. Higher costs mean less shipping volume through the strait, less trade overall, and lower actual toll revenue than the sticker-price math suggests — the same dynamic already crushing Iran's own attempts to extract fees from a war-disrupted strait.

Market Brief
President Xi Jinping to attend opening of 2026 World AI Conference and High-Level Meeting on Global AI Governance and deliver keynote speech

Premier Li Qiang chairs economic situation seminar with experts and business leaders

China's 15th Five-Year Plan for Consumption Expansion sets 2030 target of 60 trillion yuan in total retail sales of consumer goods

Escalating US-Iran tensions weigh on risk sentiment; global equities mostly lower, chip stocks plunge

International oil prices surge over 9%; gold and silver prices fall collectively

Top Stories

China's 15th Five-Year Plan for Consumption Expansion sets a target of approximately 60 trillion yuan in total retail sales of consumer goods by 2030, with consumption expected to further drive economic growth. The plan outlines 28 key initiatives to expand consumption, including supporting local governments in adjusting and optimizing real estate policies based on regional conditions, and maintaining a stable and positive trend in capital markets. Nine special focus areas are highlighted, covering silver economy, childcare products and services, cultural and tourism consumption, health consumption, and automotive consumption, with specific implementation measures for each.

US President Trump posted on social media that he will reimpose a "blockade targeting Iran," restricting only Iranian vessels or customers from entering or exiting the Strait of Hormuz, while allowing all other nations to use the strait fairly and openly. Trump also stated that the United States will impose a 20% fee on all goods transported through the strait, with implementation procedures and deployments to begin immediately.

US President Trump said the United States will continue striking Iran. He has formally notified Congress that hostilities with Iran have resumed. The US military announced the commencement of a maritime blockade against Iran at 16:00 Eastern Time on July 14. Iran warned it will not permit US interference in the management of the Strait of Hormuz and cautioned that if conflict expands in the region, it will engulf all countries in the area.

A report triggered a "bloodbath" – South Korea's stock market hit circuit breakers twice on July 13. Korea Investment Securities (KIS), the country's second-largest brokerage, released a report estimating SK Hynix's second-quarter operating profit at 60.4 trillion won, about 8% below consensus expectations, while also cutting its operating profit forecasts for this year and next. Concerns over a peak in the memory chip cycle reignited, sending SK Hynix shares plunging more than 15% – their biggest single-day drop on record. Samsung Electronics followed suit, falling over 10%, dragging the Korea Composite Stock Price Index (KOSPI) to trigger downside circuit breakers twice, eventually closing down nearly 9%. 

The panic further spilled over to other global markets, with memory-chip-related stocks in A-shares, Japan, and the U.S. all declining. However, the Bank of Korea issued a report stating that the global semiconductor market remains in short supply, and that the current AI-driven super-cycle is expected to last for some time.

China Macro & Policy

The 2026 World AI Conference and High-Level Meeting on Global AI Governance will be held in Shanghai from July 17 to 20 under the theme “Intelligent Partners, Co-Creating the Future.” President Xi Jinping will attend the opening ceremony and deliver a keynote speech, comprehensively outlining China’s policy positions and philosophical principles on AI development and governance.

Standing Committee member of the Political Bureau of the CPC Central Committee and Premier Li Qiang chaired a seminar with economic experts and business leaders to solicit opinions on the current economic situation and next steps. Li emphasized the need to maintain strategic resolve on high-quality development, intensify countercyclical adjustments, fully utilize existing policies, and pre-study and prepare new policy measures to consolidate and expand the favorable momentum of economic stability.

The General Office of the CPC Central Committee and the General Office of the State Council issued an opinion on improving the system of natural resource asset management, proposing to explore establishing separate rights to use deep underground space. It also calls for establishing state-owned agricultural land use rights and a unified management system for state-owned natural resource asset revenues, with all income to be fully incorporated into government budgets.

The State Council issued the National Health 15th Five-Year Plan, aiming for decisive progress in building a Healthy China by 2030, with average life expectancy reaching 80 years and major health indicators entering the range of high-income countries. The plan outlines 24 key tasks across five areas, including optimizing health services for all age groups throughout the life cycle.

China Equities Markets

A-shares declined on lower volume. The SSE Index fell 2.06% to 3,913.79, the Shenzhen Component Index dropped 3.48%, the ChiNext Index slid 3.1%, the BSE 50 tumbled 6.78%, the Star 50 Index fell 3.42%, and the Wind All China Index declined 3.44%. Market turnover reached 2.83 trillion yuan. Technology and growth themes retreated broadly, with cultured diamond, superhard materials, PCB, commercial aerospace, and memory chip concepts leading losses; Chinese herbal medicine and banking stocks rose against the trend.

Hong Kong markets edged sideways. The Hang Seng Index closed up 0.16% at 24,213.72, the Hang Seng Tech Index fell 0.96%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index rose 0.33%. Semiconductors and CPO concepts led declines, while oil and gas and banking stocks rose. Southbound funds net bought over 9 billion Hong Kong dollars. ZhiPu received net buying of 4.7 billion Hong Kong dollars, while JianTao Jicengban, JianTao Group, and Yangtze Optical Fibre and Cable were sold off.

A rumor circulated after trading hours claiming that quantitative strategies in A-shares had already incorporated Korean tech stock movements into their factor models, triggering cascading reactions across Chinese tech sectors. Major quantitative hedge funds denied the claim, stating that industry usage of Korean data is extremely rare and that the entire quant sector suffered significant losses yesterday.

China Industry News

China's first AI chip utilizing software-defined architecture and 3D near-memory computing technology was unveiled in Shanghai. Built on a 14-nanometer process, the chip delivers 520 trillion floating-point operations per second, with its key innovation being a novel architecture that achieves high-end computing power without relying on advanced process nodes.

China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and three other departments issued a document promoting high-quality development of internet infrastructure resources, outlining 16 tasks across six areas. The goal is to achieve systematic breakthroughs in internet infrastructure by 2030 and to fully establish a new internet infrastructure by 2035 that is advanced, fully interconnected, high-speed, ubiquitous, and secure.

China's Ministry of Civil Affairs and 13 other departments launched a three-year action plan to expand and upgrade the rehabilitation assistive devices industry, deploying over 20 specific measures across six key tasks. Priority will be given to strengthening six to eight sub-sectors, including brain-computer interfaces, care robots, prosthetics and orthotics, and hearing aids.

The Long March 5 Y14 rocket arrived safely at the Wenchang Space Launch Center. It will join the Chang'e-7 probe, which arrived earlier, for integrated assembly and testing. All systems involved in the Chang'e-7 mission are proceeding according to schedule.

TrendForce's memory price survey forecasts that due to the traditional peak season in Q3 and continued supply reductions from manufacturers in Q4 as inventories reach low levels, overall SLC NAND prices in the second half of the year are expected to rise 120% to 170% compared to the first half, with potential for further increases.

According to China's Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, national pork and live pig prices have risen for two consecutive weeks since early July. On July 12, the wholesale price of dressed pork at Beijing's Xinfadi Market reached 14.4 yuan per kg, up 15% from July 1. Nationwide live pig prices began a sustained upward trend at the end of June, rising 12.8% over the past two weeks.

Fintech

The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) launched a pan-EU joint regulatory action targeting crypto-asset service providers (CASP), focusing on operational resilience and governance systems, with particular emphasis on crypto custody services to address post-MiCA regulatory gaps.

JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, and HSBC announced the formation of a joint network using blockchain to connect tokenized bank deposits, aiming to counter the rapid expansion of dollar stablecoins like USDT and USDC in payment and settlement. The network is scheduled to launch next year.

Thailand's central bank, in coordination with Thailand's Securities and Exchange Commission and the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), initiated a special investigation into stablecoins, focusing on large-scale flows of Tether (USDT) and cracking down on market transactions designed to evade financial regulation via stablecoins.

Corporate News & Updates

Tech giant Meta significantly expanded its data center project in Louisiana, increasing its total investment from the initially announced $10 billion to as much as $50 billion, achieving a computing capacity of 5 gigawatts—placing it among the world's largest AI data centers.

Samsung Electronics' wafer foundry chief engineer, Kim Jeong-kun (James Kim), said Tesla's next-generation AI chip, AI5, has completed tape-out and is entering the final stage of mass production preparation. The chip will use Samsung's 2-nanometer process and marks the first large-scale commercial application of Samsung's 2-nanometer node.

Surging AI demand has triggered a price surge across the semiconductor supply chain. TSMC plans to raise prices for mature-node wafer foundry services starting January 2027, its first such increase in over three years, with an expected single-digit percentage hike. TSMC reported June revenue of approximately 442.68 billion new Taiwan dollars, up 67.9% year-over-year.

Jieyue Xingcheng officially launched STEPX Neo, the world's first agentic phone powered by a native large model operating system, Step AOS, featuring the Amoo agentic AI. The device aims to create a new AI "species" integrating model, software, and hardware.

Led by California's Attorney General, multiple US state attorneys general filed an antitrust lawsuit to block Paramount's $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery. The deal has received approval from all major jurisdictions except the EU. Paramount is considering relocating its headquarters out of California in response to regulatory pressure.

Volkswagen Group CEO Oliver Blume stated in an internal memo that the company still needs to cut 50,000 more jobs. Last week, Volkswagen's plan for further layoffs and factory closures in Germany was rejected by its supervisory board, signaling escalating internal conflict over cost-cutting and restructuring.

Hyundai Motor's labor union failed to reach a wage agreement and announced a partial strike for three consecutive days starting July 13. This marks the union's second consecutive year of strike action since 2025. Industry estimates suggest the strike will cost over 18.7 billion won in production losses per hour.

Global Macro & Policy

Federal Reserve Governor Waller said if this week's core inflation data again shows elevated levels, the Fed will need to consider near-term rate hikes.

Driven by increased tax revenue from growth in the AI chip industry, South Korea will set a record-high spending budget of over 800 trillion won for fiscal year 2027. National tax revenue is expected to exceed 500 trillion won, also a record high. Three major "mega projects"—chips, AI data centers, and physical AI—will receive the highest fiscal priority.

Iran's customs authority issued an order lifting emergency-era bans on exports of chemicals, polymers, and petrochemical products, but export controls on "industrial tallow" and "sulfonic acid" remain in place.

The European Union will propose a new plan this summer to enhance child online safety, focusing on setting a minimum age for social media use. Reports suggest the EU plans to officially announce a full regulatory framework in September, explicitly prohibiting social media use by children under 15.

Global Equity Markets

US equities closed lower. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.26% to 52,498.64, the S&P 500 dropped 0.79% to 7,515.34, and the Nasdaq Composite declined 1.55% to 25,873.18. Nvidia and Boeing led losses on the Dow, both down over 3%. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index fell 0.96%, Tesla dropped over 3%, Meta fell nearly 2%, and SpaceX slid over 4%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index dipped 0.14%, while Lu Kong and Hesaitech fell over 6%. Memory and semiconductor stocks led declines, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index down 4.78%, SanDisk down over 12%, and SK Hynix down over 9%.

Most major Asian markets closed lower. The KOSPI index fell 8.95% to 6,806.93, down over 27% from its record high. SK Hynix dropped 15.37%, Samsung Electronics fell 10.7%. The Nikkei 225 declined 1.92% to 67,242.73; Kioxia plunged nearly 13%. India's S&P BSE SENSEX closed up 0.06% at 77,616.40.

South Korea's financial regulators are conducting a comprehensive review of single-stock leveraged ETFs, having required asset managers to submit proposals to mitigate market volatility. Authorities will begin drafting a formal volatility suppression plan after gathering industry feedback.

The surge in leveraged lending into Korean equities is facing forced cooling. South Korea's five largest commercial banks have already used over 85% of their annual household loan growth quotas in the first half of the year, with two exceeding their limits. Credit growth will face a sharp contraction in the second half, threatening a significant reduction in leveraged equity investments funded by loans.

According to insiders, the Japanese government currently has no immediate plans to adjust the asset allocation targets of its national pension fund, but may increase domestic asset allocation within existing limits. Previously, Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama's remarks on boosting domestic allocations triggered a rise in the yen and Japanese government bonds.

Bonds

China's interbank bond market traded weakly, with yields on major government bonds rising, particularly for one-year tenors. Treasury futures closed lower, with the 30-year contract down 0.3%. The People's Bank of China conducted 224 billion yuan in reverse repo operations, net injecting 217 billion yuan, ending a 10-day streak of net withdrawals. Liquidity in the interbank market remained generally stable.

Multiple bank treasury and bond market professionals said that since July, some institutions received guidance prohibiting repurchase transactions below a 0.50% yield. The surge in AI-related bond issuance is placing significant pressure on the bond market. US Treasury yields rose across the curve. Simultaneously, Russian oil output fell to its lowest level in two and a half years due to infrastructure attacks, further tightening supply expectations.

Nigeria's Crude Oil Production Reaches Highest Level in Four Years

Nigeria's crude oil production climbed to its highest level since 2020 in June, according to the country's upstream regulator. The increase reflects improved operational efficiency, stronger production across key oil fields, and ongoing efforts to curb crude oil theft and pipeline disruptions.

Higher output is expected to strengthen export earnings, improve foreign exchange inflows, and boost government revenue at a time when fiscal sustainability remains a priority. If production continues to rise, Nigeria could improve its position within OPEC while creating a more stable environment for investment across the energy sector.
The challenge now is maintaining production growth while accelerating investment in refining, gas development, and energy infrastructure.
